text: The application shall quote the following characters if they are to represent themselves: | & ; < > ( ) $ ` \ " ' <space> <tab> <newline> and the following might need to be quoted under certain circumstances.

tests.test: double-quoting preserves literal pipe semicolon angle parens
quoting preserves literal space and tab in single argument
backslash-newline is line continuation not literal newline

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2 Quoting of the Shell Command Language. It specifies two sets of characters with respect to quoting:

Set 1 — Characters that SHALL be quoted to represent themselves:
  |  &  ;  <  >  (  )  $  `  \  "  '  <space>  <tab>  <newline>

Set 2 — Characters that MIGHT need quoting under certain circumstances:
  *  ?  [  ]  ^  -  !  #  ~  =  %  {  ,  }

The spec note says a future version may extend when Set 2 characters are special, so applications should quote them when literal meaning is intended (except '-' which is in the portable filename character set).

Section 2.2 describes four quoting mechanisms: backslash escape, single-quotes, double-quotes, and dollar-single-quotes. The here-document is another form of quoting.

What to test:

1. Each Set 1 character, when quoted (via any quoting mechanism), should pass through literally rather than being interpreted as a special character. For each character, verify that:
   a) Backslash-quoting preserves the literal value (e.g. \| produces literal |)
   b) Single-quoting preserves the literal value (e.g. '|' produces literal |)
   c) Double-quoting preserves the literal value where applicable (e.g. "|" produces literal |). Note that $, `, and \ retain special meaning inside double-quotes per 2.2.3.

2. Each Set 1 character, when NOT quoted, should trigger its special meaning (to confirm that quoting is indeed necessary). For example:
   - Unquoted | creates a pipeline
   - Unquoted & creates an asynchronous list
   - Unquoted ; separates commands
   - Unquoted < and > perform redirection
   - Unquoted ( and ) create a subshell
   - Unquoted $ introduces parameter expansion
   - Unquoted ` introduces command substitution
   - Unquoted \ escapes the next character
   - Unquoted space/tab perform word splitting
   - Unquoted newline terminates a command

3. Set 2 characters that are sometimes special should be passable literally when quoted:
   - # when quoted is not a comment
   - * and ? when quoted are not glob patterns
   - [ ] when quoted are not bracket expressions
   - ~ when quoted is not tilde expansion
   - ! when quoted in interactive shells is not history expansion

Observable behavior: Use echo or printf to output each character and verify stdout contains exactly the literal character. For characters that affect control flow (|, &, ;, newline), verify the quoting prevents the control flow behavior by checking that the character appears in the output.

Preconditions: None beyond a working POSIX shell. Some Set 2 tests (! for history expansion) require an interactive shell.

text: A <backslash> that is not quoted shall preserve the literal value of the following character, with the exception of a <newline>.

tests.test: backslash preserves literal value of following character
backslash escapes semicolon so it is literal
backslash escapes space preventing field split

testing_instructions:
This requirement comes from section 2.2.1 (Escape Character) in the shell command language. When a backslash is unquoted, it quotes exactly the next character so that character is treated literally, except when the next character is newline.

Test objective:

Confirm that an unquoted backslash preserves the literal value of the immediately following non-newline character during token recognition.

What to test:

1. Escaped shell control/operator characters are literal
   - Use an unquoted backslash before characters such as ';', '|', '&', '<', '>', '(', ')', and '#' and verify they are handled as ordinary data characters in a word, not as syntax.
   - Observable result: output contains the literal character and no operator side effect occurs (no command split, pipeline, redirection, grouping, or comment start caused by that escaped character).

2. Escaped expansion introducers are literal
   - Use an unquoted backslash before '$' and '`' and verify parameter expansion and command substitution are not triggered by that escaped character.
   - Observable result: output includes the literal '$' or '`' character where escaped.

3. Escaped blanks stay within one token
   - Use an unquoted backslash before a space (and optionally tab) and verify the escaped blank is part of the same argument rather than delimiting words.
   - Observable result: argument count/content demonstrates a single token containing the blank.

4. Escaped backslash yields literal backslash
   - Verify that '\\' in unquoted context yields a literal backslash character.

Scope notes:

- Do not use backslash-newline behavior as evidence for this requirement; that exception is covered by the separate line-continuation requirement.
- Prefer assertions on observable parsing outcomes (stdout/stderr text, argument boundaries, control-flow side effects), not implementation internals.

text: If a <newline> immediately follows the <backslash>, the shell shall interpret this as line continuation.
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.1 (Escape Character) of the shell command language. It defines the special exception to ordinary backslash escaping: when an unquoted backslash is immediately followed by newline, the pair is treated as line continuation.

Test objective:

Verify that backslash-newline is interpreted as continuation of the same logical input line, not as a literal backslash/newline sequence.

What to test:

1. Command continuation across physical lines
   - Split a command name or command text across a backslash-newline boundary and verify the shell parses and executes it as a single command line.
   - Observable result: command executes successfully as if written on one line.

2. Continuation between words/tokens in one command
   - Place backslash-newline between command arguments/tokens and verify parsing continues with the next physical line as part of the same command.
   - Observable result: stdout reflects the intended arguments with no command break introduced by the escaped newline.

3. Repeated continuations
   - Use multiple consecutive backslash-newline pairs and verify continuation semantics apply repeatedly.
   - Observable result: shell still produces the same command/token sequence as the equivalent single-line form.

Scope and interaction notes:

- This requirement is specifically about interpretation of backslash-newline as continuation; literal-preservation behavior for backslash followed by non-newline characters is covered separately.
- Assertions should focus on observable parser behavior: command execution success, output text, and absence of an unintended command terminator at the escaped newline.

text: The <backslash> and <newline> shall be removed before splitting the input into tokens.

tests.test: line continuation: backslash-newline removed before tokenizing

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.1 (Escape Character). When an unquoted backslash is immediately followed by newline, both characters are removed before tokenization.

Test objective:

Verify that the backslash-newline pair does not appear in token input and cannot influence token boundaries as a blank or separator.

What to test:

1. Removal before token splitting
   - Place backslash-newline inside what should become one word (for example, splitting a word across physical lines).
   - Observable result: the resulting token is the concatenation with no inserted character.

2. No token separator introduced
   - Use a form where token boundaries would change if whitespace/newline remained.
   - Observable result: argument count and content match the equivalent single-line command.

3. Works repeatedly
   - Use multiple backslash-newline pairs in a row.
   - Observable result: all pairs are removed, and parsing matches the same command written without those physical newlines.

Scope note:

- This requirement concerns removal and tokenization effects of backslash-newline; the distinct requirement about interpreting it as line continuation is covered separately.

text: Enclosing characters in single-quotes ( '' ) shall preserve the literal value of each character within the single-quotes.

tests.test: single quotes preserve all characters literally

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.2 (Single-Quotes). Inside single-quotes, every character is literal.

Test objective:

Verify that characters enclosed in single-quotes are passed unchanged to the utility and are not interpreted by shell expansion, substitution, globbing, or escaping rules.

What to test:

1. Literal preservation of special characters
   - Include characters that are normally special to the shell (for example '$', '*', '?', '[', ']', '#', and backslash) inside single-quotes.
   - Observable result: output contains those exact literal characters.

2. No expansion or substitution inside single-quotes
   - Include parameter-like and command-substitution-like text (for example '$var' and '`cmd`') inside single-quotes.
   - Observable result: no expansion/substitution occurs; literal text is preserved.

3. Contrast with unquoted behavior
   - Where practical, compare with an unquoted form that would expand or pattern-match.
   - Observable result: quoted form remains literal while unquoted form shows shell interpretation.

Scope note:

- This requirement only covers characters within single-quoted regions. Handling of an actual single-quote character itself requires leaving and re-entering quoting and is outside this exact sentence.

text: Enclosing characters in double-quotes ( "" ) shall preserve the literal value of all characters within the double-quotes, with the exception of the characters backquote, <dollar-sign>, and <backslash>, as follows: $ The <dollar-sign> shall retain its special meaning introducing parameter expansion (see 2.6.2 Parameter Expansion ), a form of command substitution (see 2.6.3 Command Substitution ), and arithmetic expansion (see 2.6.4 Arithmetic Expansion ), but shall not retain its special meaning introducing the dollar-single-quotes form of quoting (see 2.2.4 Dollar-Single-Quotes ).

tests.test: alias quoting handles single quotes in value
backslash dollar and backquote inside braces
backslash in double quotes special only before certain chars
double quotes allow parameter and command and arithmetic expansion

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes). Double-quotes preserve literal value of characters except backquote, dollar-sign, and backslash. In particular, '$' keeps its expansion-introducing roles (parameter expansion, command substitution, arithmetic expansion) but does not introduce dollar-single-quotes syntax.

Test objective:

Verify the baseline semantics of double-quotes: mostly literal preservation, with continued special handling for '$', backquote, and backslash as specified in 2.2.3.

What to test:

1. Literal preservation for ordinary characters
   - Place whitespace and shell metacharacters that would otherwise split or glob inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: they are preserved literally in the produced argument(s).

2. '$' remains special for expansions
   - In double-quotes, exercise parameter expansion, '$(...)' command substitution, and '$((...))' arithmetic expansion.
   - Observable result: all three expansions occur.

3. '$' does not start dollar-single-quote quoting inside double-quotes
   - Include text like $'...'
   - Observable result: this is not treated as dollar-single-quote syntax in double-quoted context.

4. Backquote/backslash exceptions are covered by their detailed sub-rules
   - Ensure behavior remains consistent with section rules for backquote command substitution and backslash handling in double-quotes.

Scope note:

- This is the umbrella obligation for double-quotes; more specific obligations in subsequent requirements (2.2.3-008 onward) refine exact behavior in nested contexts and escape cases.

text: The input characters within the quoted string that are also enclosed between "$(" and the matching ')' shall not be affected by the double-quotes, but rather shall define the command(s) whose output replaces the "$(...)" when the word is expanded.

tests.test: inner double quotes inside command substitution
string starting with = is just a WORD

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes). It states that characters inside a '$(...)' region that appears within double-quotes are governed by command-substitution parsing, not by the surrounding double-quotes.

Test objective:

Verify that the command text inside '$(...)' retains its own syntax and is not neutralized by outer double-quotes.

What to test:

1. Inner quoting and syntax inside '$(...)'
   - Use double-quotes and other shell syntax inside the command substitution while the whole construct is inside outer double-quotes.
   - Observable result: inner command executes according to normal command-substitution rules and produces expected output.

2. Outer double-quotes still apply to substitution result
   - Place substitution result where field-splitting/globbing could happen if unquoted.
   - Observable result: result is inserted as part of a double-quoted word, preserving expected quoting behavior for the expanded text.

3. Distinguish outer and inner quoting scopes
   - Ensure syntax validity depends on matching/closing inside '$(...)', not on the outer quote context.

Scope note:

- This requirement is specifically about interpretation of the command text between '$(' and matching ')' while nested in a double-quoted string.

text: The tokenizing rules in 2.3 Token Recognition shall be applied recursively to find the matching ')' .
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 and references section 2.3 token recognition. When parsing '$(...)', tokenizing must be applied recursively to locate the correct matching ')'.

Test objective:

Verify that matching of the closing ')' for command substitution is determined by recursive parsing, not by the first literal ')' byte encountered.

What to test:

1. Nested parentheses/constructs inside '$(...)'
   - Use inner constructs that contain parentheses or nested substitutions.
   - Observable result: parser selects the structurally correct closing ')' and command substitution succeeds.

2. Quoted parentheses inside '$(...)'
   - Include ')' characters in quoted strings within the substitution.
   - Observable result: quoted ')' does not prematurely terminate the substitution.

3. Correct boundary of substitution
   - Append text immediately after the command substitution.
   - Observable result: only text up to the true matching ')' belongs to substitution; following text remains outside.

Scope note:

- This requirement concerns parsing of substitution boundaries, not the runtime behavior of the substituted command itself beyond being sufficient to confirm correct boundary detection.

text: For the four varieties of parameter expansion that provide for substring processing (see 2.6.2 Parameter Expansion ), within the string of characters from an enclosed "${" to the matching '}' , the double-quotes within which the expansion occurs shall have no effect on the handling of any special characters.

tests.test: substring processing not affected by outer double quotes

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 and section 2.6.2 parameter expansion. For the four substring-processing parameter expansion forms, outer double-quotes do not affect handling of special characters within the '${...}' expression body.

Test objective:

Verify that special characters used by substring-processing parameter expansions are interpreted by parameter-expansion rules even when the expansion appears inside double-quotes.

What to test:

1. Substring-processing operator semantics under outer double-quotes
   - Use representative substring-processing forms and patterns inside '${...}' while the whole expansion is inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: matching/removal behavior follows substring-processing rules, not literalization by outer double-quotes.

2. Special characters within substring patterns
   - Include pattern metacharacters in the substring-processing expression.
   - Observable result: they retain their parameter-expansion meaning in that context.

3. Contrast with non-substring forms
   - Where practical, compare with parameter-expansion forms that are governed by the separate non-substring rule.

Scope note:

- This requirement applies only to the four substring-processing varieties; other parameter expansion varieties are covered separately.

text: For parameter expansions other than the four varieties that provide for substring processing, within the string of characters from an enclosed "${" to the matching '}' , the double-quotes within which the expansion occurs shall preserve the literal value of all characters, with the exception of the characters double-quote, backquote, <dollar-sign>, and <backslash>.

tests.test: double quotes preserve expansion result literally

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 and applies to parameter expansions other than the four substring-processing varieties. In these cases, inside '${...}' and within outer double-quotes, characters are preserved literally except double-quote, backquote, dollar-sign, and backslash.

Test objective:

Verify that non-substring parameter expansion forms inside double-quotes preserve literal characters in expansion context except for the listed exceptions.

What to test:

1. Literal preservation of ordinary/special non-exception characters
   - Use non-substring '${...}' forms with characters that might otherwise be treated specially.
   - Observable result: those characters are treated literally in this context.

2. Exception characters still active by rule
   - Exercise cases involving '"', '`', '$', and '\\' in relevant positions.
   - Observable result: behavior follows their specified special handling, not blanket literal preservation.

3. Expanded result remains quoted by outer double-quotes
   - Validate observable output/argument preservation (for example no glob expansion or field splitting of expanded text).

Scope note:

- This requirement excludes substring-processing varieties, which have distinct handling rules.

text: The backquote and <dollar-sign> characters shall follow the same rules as for characters in double-quotes described in this section.
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes). It says backquote and dollar-sign follow the same rules already defined for characters in double-quotes.

Test objective:

Verify that within double-quotes, both '$' and backquote retain their command/expansion-related behavior according to the section rules.

What to test:

1. Dollar-sign behavior in double-quotes
   - Exercise parameter expansion and '$(...)' command substitution in double-quoted context.
   - Observable result: expansions occur as specified.

2. Backquote behavior in double-quotes
   - Use backquoted command substitution within double-quotes.
   - Observable result: command substitution executes and its output is substituted.

3. Quoting context consistency
   - Ensure both forms behave according to double-quote semantics (for example resulting text remains in double-quoted word context).

Scope note:

- This requirement is a linkage rule to the detailed '$' and backquote rules in the same section; tests should confirm those behaviors are active in double-quoted text.

text: The <backslash> character shall follow the same rules as for characters in double-quotes described in this section except that it shall additionally retain its special meaning as an escape character when followed by '}' and this shall prevent the escaped '}' from being considered when determining the matching '}' (using the rule in 2.6.2 Parameter Expansion ).
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3. In double-quotes, backslash generally follows double-quote backslash rules, and additionally escapes '}' so that an escaped '}' is ignored for determining the matching '}' of '${...}'.

Test objective:

Verify the special '\}' behavior in double-quoted parameter expansion and ensure backslash handling aligns with the section's double-quote rules.

What to test:

1. Escaped closing brace inside '${...}'
   - Construct a parameter expansion where '\}' appears before the true closing brace while inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: escaped '}' does not terminate expansion; parser continues to the correct matching unescaped '}'.

2. Boundary correctness and successful parse
   - Include trailing text after the expansion to confirm where expansion ends.
   - Observable result: expansion boundary is at the intended closing brace and command parses/executes as expected.

3. General double-quote backslash behavior remains intact
   - Verify no regression against normal backslash special-follow set in double-quotes.

Scope note:

- This requirement is narrowly about the extra '\}' rule for matching-brace determination in parameter expansion.

text: The backquote shall retain its special meaning introducing the other form of command substitution (see 2.6.3 Command Substitution).
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 and 2.6.3. It states that backquote retains its role introducing the backquoted form of command substitution.

Test objective:

Verify that backquoted command substitution is recognized and executed where this section applies (including in double-quoted contexts).

What to test:

1. Basic backquoted substitution
   - Use `...` command substitution and confirm output replacement behavior.
   - Observable result: substituted text equals command stdout (subject to command substitution newline-trimming rules).

2. Backquote remains special in double-quotes
   - Place backquoted substitution inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: substitution still occurs and result participates in double-quoted word context.

3. Interaction with nested shell syntax
   - Use a representative nested form (for example with here-document or nested quoting where valid) to ensure parser still treats backquote as substitution introducer.

Scope note:

- This requirement is about recognizing backquote as command-substitution introducer; detailed edge cases for undefined forms are covered by adjacent rules.

text: Outside of "$(...)" and "${...}" the <backslash> shall retain its special meaning as an escape character (see 2.2.1 Escape Character (Backslash)) only when immediately followed by one of the following characters: $ ` " \ <newline>.

tests.test: backslash in double quotes special only before certain chars
escaped double quote inside double quotes

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes), with references to 2.2.1, 2.6.4, and 2.7.4. In double-quoted context, outside "$(...)" and "${...}", backslash acts as an escape character only when immediately followed by '$', '`', '\\', newline, or by a double-quote that is special in the cited contexts.

Test objective:

Verify the restricted set of characters for which backslash is special inside double-quotes, and verify that for other following characters backslash is preserved literally.

What to test:

1. Special-follow set inside double-quotes
   - Check backslash before '$', '`', '"', '\\', and newline inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result:
     - '\$' yields literal '$' (no parameter expansion trigger from that escaped character).
     - '\`' yields literal backquote (no backquote substitution trigger from that escaped character).
     - '\\' yields a single literal backslash.
     - '\"' yields a literal double-quote included in the quoted word.
     - backslash-newline is treated as line continuation (pair removed), not literal data.

2. Non-special following characters
   - Use backslash before characters not in the allowed set (for example 'n' in "\n").
   - Observable result: backslash is not consumed as an escape; both characters are preserved (subject to normal quote removal semantics) and the utility receives literal text.

3. Outside nested "$(...)" and "${...}" scope
   - Ensure tested escapes are in double-quoted text not inside command substitution or parameter expansion braces.
   - Observable result: behavior matches this rule's scope; nested contexts are governed by their own rules.

Scope note:

- This requirement is about backslash behavior specifically in double-quoted text outside "$(...)" and "${...}". Detailed '${...}' brace-handling exceptions are covered by adjacent requirements.

text: When double-quotes are used to quote a parameter expansion, command substitution, or arithmetic expansion, the literal value of all characters within the result of the expansion shall be preserved.

tests.test: double quotes preserve expansion result literally

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes). It applies when a parameter expansion, command substitution, or arithmetic expansion occurs inside double-quotes: the resulting expanded text is preserved literally.

Test objective:

Verify that expansion results produced inside double-quotes do not undergo field splitting or pathname expansion, and remain a single quoted field (subject only to command substitution trailing-newline handling defined elsewhere).

What to test:

1. Parameter expansion result preservation
   - Expand a variable whose value contains spaces and glob characters inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: output retains those characters literally and as part of one argument (no glob expansion / no split).

2. Command substitution result preservation in double-quotes
   - Use "$(...)" inside double-quotes with output containing spaces or wildcard characters.
   - Observable result: substituted text is preserved literally in the quoted field.

3. Arithmetic expansion in double-quotes
   - Use "$((...))" inside double-quotes with surrounding text.
   - Observable result: arithmetic result is inserted without causing additional token splitting.

4. Contrast with unquoted expansion
   - Where practical, compare with the same expansion unquoted.
   - Observable result: unquoted form may split/expand while double-quoted form preserves literal value.

Scope note:

- This requirement is about handling of expansion results in double-quoted context; it does not redefine the expansion-specific computation rules themselves.

text: The application shall ensure that a double-quote that is not within "$(...)" nor within "${...}" is immediately preceded by a <backslash> in order to be included within double-quotes.

tests.test: double quotes backslash produces single backslash
escaped double quote inside double quotes

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.3 (Double-Quotes). To include a literal double-quote character within a double-quoted string (outside "$(...)" and "${...}"), the application must escape that quote with a preceding backslash.

Test objective:

Verify that an unescaped double-quote ends double-quote context, and that a backslash-escaped double-quote is included as literal data within the double-quoted word.

What to test:

1. Escaped double-quote inclusion
   - Use \" inside double-quotes.
   - Observable result: output contains a literal " character and command parses successfully.

2. Structural role of unescaped double-quote
   - Demonstrate that an unescaped " acts as a quoting delimiter, not a literal data byte.
   - Observable result: quoted region boundaries follow unescaped quotes; include a negative/syntax-shape check where practical.

3. Scope restriction
   - Keep the tested quote outside nested "$(...)" and "${...}" contexts.
   - Observable result: behavior matches this rule; nested contexts are covered by their own parsing rules.

Scope note:

- This requirement imposes an application-side constraint for portable scripts; tests should emphasize the observable inclusion of literal quotes when escaped and delimiter behavior when not escaped.

text: If any unescaped double-quote characters occur within the string, other than in embedded command substitutions, the behavior is unspecified.
untestable_reason: Behavior is explicitly unspecified by POSIX; no single portable expected outcome can be asserted across conforming shells.

text: A quoted (single-quoted, double-quoted, or dollar-single-quoted) string that begins, but does not end, within the ``"`...`"`` sequence produces undefined results.
untestable_reason: Behavior is explicitly undefined by POSIX; portable pass/fail assertions are not possible.

text: A ``"`...`"`` sequence that begins, but does not end, within the same double-quoted string produces undefined results.
untestable_reason: Behavior is explicitly undefined by POSIX; portable pass/fail assertions are not possible.

text: A sequence of characters starting with a <dollar-sign> immediately followed by a single-quote ( $' ) shall preserve the literal value of all characters up to an unescaped terminating single-quote ( ' ), with the exception of certain <backslash>-escape sequences, as follows: \" yields a <quotation-mark> (double-quote) character, but note that <quotation-mark> can be included unescaped.

tests.test: backslash preserves literal value of following character

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.4 (Dollar-Single-Quotes). It defines the base behavior of the $'...' quoting form: text is literal up to the terminating unescaped single-quote, except for the listed backslash-escape sequences.

Test objective:

Verify that $'...' forms one quoted word, preserves literal characters generally, and applies the documented escape-sequence exceptions.

What to test:

1. Base quoting behavior
   - Characters inside $'...' are preserved as data until the terminating unescaped single-quote.
   - Observable result: output matches literal content when no escape processing applies.

2. Escapes exception path is active
   - Include a documented escape (for example \" or \n) and verify converted output appears.
   - Observable result: converted bytes/characters are emitted instead of raw backslash-sequence text.

3. Tokenization/word behavior
   - Ensure $'...' contributes a single shell word in command input.

Scope note:

- Detailed per-escape obligations are covered by the individual requirements in this section; this requirement is the umbrella rule introducing the construct.

text: In cases where a variable number of characters can be used to specify an escape sequence ( \x XX and \ ddd ), the escape sequence shall be terminated by the first character that is not of the expected type or, for \ ddd sequences, when the maximum number of characters specified has been found, whichever occurs first.
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.4. For variable-length escape forms (\xXX and \ddd), parsing of the escape must stop at the first non-matching character, or at the octal maximum length for \ddd.

Test objective:

Verify correct termination boundaries for variable-length escapes in $'...'.

What to test:

1. Hex termination by first non-hex character
   - Example shape: $'\x41Z'.
   - Observable result: yields 'A' followed by literal 'Z'.

2. Octal termination by max length
   - Example shape: $'\1012'.
   - Observable result: \101 yields 'A', with trailing '2' left as regular text.

3. Correct value generation and boundary preservation
   - Verify both converted byte and leftover suffix bytes are correct.

text: These <backslash>-escape sequences shall be processed (replaced with the bytes or characters they yield) immediately prior to word expansion (see 2.6 Word Expansions ) of the word in which the dollar-single-quotes sequence occurs.

tests.test: single quotes preserve all characters literally

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.4 and references 2.6 Word Expansions. Documented $'...' escape sequences are processed immediately before word expansion of the containing word.

Test objective:

Verify escape replacement occurs at the required phase and that resulting bytes participate in normal subsequent word processing.

What to test:

1. Escape replacement occurs for $'...'
   - Use sequences such as \n and \x41 and verify converted output.

2. Processing order consistency
   - Ensure behavior matches an already-converted word by the time expansion effects are observable.

3. Observable outputs only
   - Validate via stdout/byte checks rather than implementation internals.

text: However, implementations shall not replace an unsupported character with bytes that do not form valid characters in that locale's character set.
untestable_reason: Portable verification requires locale-dependent unsupported-character scenarios and encoding-validity checks that are implementation-specific.
testing_instructions:
This requirement is locale- and implementation-dependent. If an escape (notably \e or \cX) denotes a character unsupported in the active locale, implementations must not emit byte sequences that are invalid characters in that locale.

Testing guidance:

- This is not portable for conformance automation in this matrix because unsupported-character cases and validity checks vary by locale/implementation.
- If tested locally, use implementation-specific locale fixtures and byte-validation tooling.

text: If a <backslash>-escape sequence represents a single-quote character (for example \' ), that sequence shall not terminate the dollar-single-quote sequence.
testing_instructions:
This requirement is from section 2.2.4. An escape sequence that denotes a single-quote (for example \\') must not terminate the enclosing $'...' quote.

Test objective:

Verify escaped apostrophe remains data and parsing continues within the same dollar-single-quote sequence.

What to test:

1. Internal escaped apostrophe
   - Example shape: $'can\'t'.
   - Observable result: output contains apostrophe and following characters from same quoted sequence.

2. No premature quote termination
   - Ensure parser does not end $'...' at the escaped quote.

text: The behavior of an unescaped <backslash> immediately followed by any other character, including <newline>, is unspecified.
untestable_reason: POSIX marks behavior unspecified; no portable expected output exists.
testing_instructions: No conformance assertion should be made; behavior is intentionally unspecified.

text: If a \xXX or \ddd escape sequence yields a byte whose value is 0, it is unspecified whether that null byte is included in the result or if that byte and any following regular characters and escape sequences up to the terminating unescaped single-quote are evaluated and discarded.
untestable_reason: POSIX marks null-byte handling in this case unspecified; portable pass/fail is not possible.
testing_instructions: No conformance assertion should be made; behavior is intentionally unspecified.

text: If the octal value specified by \ddd will not fit in a byte, the results are unspecified.
untestable_reason: POSIX marks overflow handling unspecified; portable expected behavior is not defined.
testing_instructions: No conformance assertion should be made; behavior is intentionally unspecified.

text: If a \e or \cX escape sequence specifies a character that does not have an encoding in the locale in effect when these <backslash>-escape sequences are processed, the result is implementation-defined.
untestable_reason: Outcome is implementation-defined and locale-dependent; no single portable expectation can be asserted.
testing_instructions: Treat this as implementation-defined behavior. Any checks must be implementation-specific and should not be used as strict cross-shell conformance assertions.

text: The shell shall read its input in terms of lines. (For details about how the shell reads its input, see the description of sh .) The input lines can be of unlimited length.

tests.test: end of input delimits current token

testing_instructions: This requirement establishes line-oriented shell input in token recognition mode. Test with multi-line scripts and confirm each physical line is read and processed in sequence, including final lines without extra delimiters. Observable behavior should come from command outputs and parsing outcomes, not implementation internals.

text: These lines shall be parsed using two major modes: ordinary token recognition and processing of here-documents.
testing_instructions: Verify that token recognition alternates between ordinary token parsing and here-document processing when appropriate. Use scripts that contain both ordinary commands and here-documents, and confirm parsing follows the correct mode for each region. Observable behavior is successful command execution with expected here-document handling.

text: When an io_here token has been recognized by the grammar (see 2.10 Shell Grammar ), one or more of the subsequent lines immediately following the next NEWLINE token form the body of a here-document and shall be parsed according to the rules of 2.7.4 Here-Document .
testing_instructions: Test that after recognizing an io_here token, lines after the next NEWLINE are treated as here-document body and parsed under 2.7.4 rules. Include delimiter matching and body content with quoting/expansion variations. Confirm resulting command input and output match here-document semantics.

text: Any non- NEWLINE tokens (including more io_here tokens) that are recognized while searching for the next NEWLINE token shall be saved for processing after the here-document has been parsed.
testing_instructions: Verify non-NEWLINE tokens discovered while searching for the NEWLINE that starts here-document processing are deferred for later processing. Use commands containing additional tokens and multiple here-documents on one line. Confirm deferred tokens are interpreted after the proper here-document body is consumed.

text: If a saved token is an io_here token, the corresponding here-document shall start on the line immediately following the line containing the trailing delimiter of the previous here-document.
testing_instructions: For multiple saved io_here tokens, verify each subsequent here-document starts immediately after the previous here-document trailing delimiter line. Use chained here-doc redirections in one command line. Confirm ordering and body association are preserved.

text: When it is not processing an io_here , the shell shall break its input into tokens by applying the first applicable rule below to each character in turn in its input.

tests.test: end of input delimits current token

testing_instructions: Outside io_here mode, verify input is tokenized by applying the first applicable token-recognition rule character-by-character. Use inputs where different rule precedence choices would change parsing. Confirm observed command structure follows POSIX rule ordering.

text: At the start of input or after a previous token has just been delimited, the first or next token, respectively, shall start with the first character that has not already been included in a token and is not discarded according to the rules below.

tests.test: end of input delimits current token

testing_instructions: Verify token start positions at beginning of input and after delimiters use the first not-yet-consumed, not-discarded character. Use cases with discarded blanks/comments and adjacent operators/words. Confirm resulting token boundaries and command behavior are correct.

text: Once a token has started, zero or more characters from the input shall be appended to the token until the end of the token is delimited according to one of the rules below.

tests.test: end of substitution does not delimit token

testing_instructions: Test that once a token starts, characters keep appending until a delimiting rule applies. Use suffix-attachment cases (for example substitution result followed by literal suffix) and verify they stay in one token where required. Confirm output reflects intact token construction.

text: When both the start and end of a token have been delimited, the characters forming the token shall be exactly those in the input between the two delimiters, including any quoting characters.

tests.test: end of substitution does not delimit token

testing_instructions: Verify a delimited token contains exactly the original input span between token start and end delimiters, including quoting characters. Use cases with embedded quotes/substitution syntax and confirm token content is preserved for later phases as specified.

text: If a rule below indicates that a token is delimited, and no characters have been included in the token, that empty token shall be discarded.
testing_instructions: Test that delimiter events with zero collected token characters produce a discarded empty token. Use repeated delimiters/blanks and verify no empty argument/word is created solely by token recognition. Confirm observable argument counts and outputs.

text: If the end of input is recognized, the current token (if any) shall be delimited.

tests.test: end of substitution does not delimit token

testing_instructions: Verify end-of-input delimits the current token if one is in progress. Use inputs where final token has no trailing blank/operator. Confirm final word is still recognized and executed correctly.

text: If the previous character was used as part of an operator and the current character is not quoted and can be used with the previous characters to form an operator, it shall be used as part of that (operator) token.

tests.test: quoted field does not delimit token

testing_instructions: Test operator growth: when current character can extend an operator token, it is appended to that operator. Use constructs like >> and verify they are recognized as single multi-character operators. Confirm redirection/operator behavior matches combined token semantics.

text: If the previous character was used as part of an operator and the current character cannot be used with the previous characters to form an operator, the operator containing the previous character shall be delimited.
testing_instructions: Verify operator delimiting when the next character cannot continue the current operator token. Use boundaries where operator and following token must split. Confirm parser treats operator and subsequent token separately.

text: If the current character is an unquoted <backslash>, single-quote, or double-quote or is the first character of an unquoted <dollar-sign> single-quote sequence, it shall affect quoting for subsequent characters up to the end of the quoted text.

tests.test: pwd -L has no unnecessary double slashes
pwd -P from deep dir has no unnecessary double slashes

testing_instructions: Test that unquoted backslash/single-quote/double-quote and unquoted $' start affect quoting state until quoted text ends. Use each quoting form and confirm subsequent characters are interpreted under the corresponding quoting rules. Observable outcomes should include literal preservation/expansion behavior expected for each quote context.

text: During token recognition no substitutions shall be actually performed, and the result token shall contain exactly the characters that appear in the input unmodified, including any embedded or enclosing quotes or substitution operators, between the start and the end of the quoted text.

tests.test: inner double quotes inside command substitution

testing_instructions: During token recognition, verify substitutions are not executed while scanning quoted regions; token text should preserve original input bytes/operators unchanged through recognition. Use substitution syntax inside quoted spans and confirm lexing boundaries are correct before expansion phase behavior appears.

text: The token shall not be delimited by the end of the quoted field.

tests.test: quoted field does not delimit token

testing_instructions: Verify reaching end of quoted field does not itself delimit the token. Use quoted text immediately followed by unquoted suffix and confirm resulting token remains contiguous. Output should demonstrate single-token concatenation.

text: If the current character is an unquoted '$' or '`' , the shell shall identify the start of any candidates for parameter expansion ( 2.6.2 Parameter Expansion ), command substitution ( 2.6.3 Command Substitution ), or arithmetic expansion ( 2.6.4 Arithmetic Expansion ) from their introductory unquoted character sequences: '$' or "${" , "$(" or '`' , and "$((" , respectively.

tests.test: double quotes allow parameter and command and arithmetic expansion

testing_instructions: Test detection of expansion candidates from unquoted '$' and '`' introducers. Include parameter, command, and arithmetic forms and verify parser identifies correct construct starts. Confirm later expansion behavior reflects recognized construct type.

text: The shell shall read sufficient input to determine the end of the unit to be expanded (as explained in the cited sections).
testing_instructions: Verify the shell reads enough input to find the full extent of each expansion unit before continuing tokenization. Use nested/complex expansions requiring lookahead. Confirm parsing succeeds with correct closing delimiters and boundaries.

text: While processing the characters, if instances of expansions or quoting are found nested within the substitution, the shell shall recursively process them in the manner specified for the construct that is found.
testing_instructions: Test recursive processing when expansions/quoting are nested inside substitution text. Use nested command substitutions and mixed quoting. Confirm resolved output and parsing order match recursive construct handling.

text: For "$(" and '`' only, if instances of io_here tokens are found nested within the substitution, they shall be parsed according to the rules of 2.7.4 Here-Document ; if the terminating ')' or '`' of the substitution occurs before the NEWLINE token marking the start of the here-document, the behavior is unspecified.

tests.test: substitution does not delimit token nested

testing_instructions: For nested io_here inside $(...) or backquotes, verify here-document parsing follows 2.7.4; additionally treat the specified early-terminator case as unspecified (no strict portable assertion). Focus conformance checks on defined nested here-doc behavior and avoid requiring a single outcome for unspecified branch.

text: The characters found from the beginning of the substitution to its end, allowing for any recursion necessary to recognize embedded constructs, shall be included unmodified in the result token, including any embedded or enclosing substitution operators or quotes.

tests.test: inner double quotes inside command substitution

testing_instructions: Verify characters spanning a substitution construct are included unmodified in the token during recognition, including embedded operators/quotes. Use nested substitution examples and confirm token-level preservation before later expansion steps.

text: The token shall not be delimited by the end of the substitution.

tests.test: substitution does not delimit token nested

testing_instructions: Test that reaching substitution end does not delimit the surrounding token. Use substitution immediately adjacent to literal suffix/prefix text and confirm single-token concatenation behavior.

text: If the current character is not quoted and can be used as the first character of a new operator, the current token (if any) shall be delimited.
testing_instructions: Verify when an unquoted character can start a new operator, current token is delimited first. Use word-then-operator cases and confirm parser splits at operator start appropriately.

text: The current character shall be used as the beginning of the next (operator) token.
testing_instructions: Test that the operator-starting character itself becomes the first character of the next operator token. Confirm resulting operator semantics (for example redirection) match correct operator tokenization.

text: If the current character is an unquoted <blank>, any token containing the previous character is delimited and the current character shall be discarded.
testing_instructions: Verify unquoted blank characters delimit any current token and are discarded, not retained as token data. Use multiple-blank separations and confirm resulting arguments/words are separated as expected.

text: If the previous character was part of a word, the current character shall be appended to that word.

tests.test: E treats punctuation as part of word

testing_instructions: When prior character is part of a word, verify current character appends to that word. Use contiguous non-delimiter characters and confirm they remain in one word token.

text: If the current character is a '#' , it and all subsequent characters up to, but excluding, the next <newline> shall be discarded as a comment.
testing_instructions: Test comment recognition at unquoted '#': characters up to but excluding next newline are discarded. Confirm preceding token content remains, comment text is ignored, and parsing resumes on next line.

text: In situations where the shell parses its input as a program , once a complete_command has been recognized by the grammar (see 2.10 Shell Grammar ), the complete_command shall be executed before the next complete_command is tokenized and parsed.

tests.test: complete_command executed before next is tokenized

testing_instructions: In program parsing mode, verify each recognized complete_command executes before tokenization/parsing of the next complete_command. Use sequential commands where earlier execution affects later parsing/expansion context. Confirm observed ordering matches this rule.

text: If any saved token includes a <newline> character, the behavior is unspecified.
untestable_reason: POSIX explicitly marks behavior unspecified; no single portable expected outcome can be asserted.
testing_instructions: Treat this clause as unspecified behavior: do not enforce one required result across shells.

text: The <newline> that ends the line is not considered part of the comment.
testing_instructions: Verify comment stripping stops before the terminating newline and parsing resumes at the next line. Use a command with trailing comment followed by another command on the next line; confirm second command executes normally.

text: The current character is used as the start of a new word.
testing_instructions: Verify rule-10 fallback behavior: when no earlier token-recognition rule applies, the current character begins a new word token. Use simple adjacent word/operator scenarios and confirm expected word starts and argument boundaries.

text: Once a token is delimited, it is categorized as required by the grammar in 2.10 Shell Grammar.
testing_instructions: Verify delimited tokens are categorized per shell grammar (for example reserved words/operators/words) after token recognition. Use grammar-sensitive constructs such as case/esac and confirm parser category decisions match expected command structure.

text: After a token has been categorized as type TOKEN (see 2.10.1 Shell Grammar Lexical Conventions ), including (recursively) any token resulting from an alias substitution, the TOKEN shall be subject to alias substitution if all of the following conditions are true: The TOKEN does not contain any quoting characters.

tests.test: alias trailing blank expands next word

testing_instructions:
This requirement is from 2.3.1 Alias Substitution. After token categorization to TOKEN (including recursively produced tokens), alias substitution is applied when the listed conditions are satisfied.

Test objective:

Verify alias substitution eligibility is decided at TOKEN stage and that quoting/no-quoting and command-position context affect whether substitution occurs.

What to test:

1. TOKEN-level substitution context
   - Use alias names in command-name position and verify substitution happens.

2. Quoting prevents substitution
   - Use quoted/escaped alias name and verify no substitution.

3. Recursive token flow
   - Use alias values that produce further tokens and verify subsequent TOKENs are considered per 2.3.1 rules.

Observable behavior should be asserted via command output and execution path (expanded alias vs literal token).

text: When a TOKEN is subject to alias substitution, the value of the alias shall be processed as if it had been read from the input instead of the TOKEN , with token recognition (see 2.3 Token Recognition ) resuming at the start of the alias value.

tests.test: substitution does not delimit token nested

testing_instructions:
When a TOKEN is subject to alias substitution, its alias value is processed as if read from input and token recognition resumes at the start of that alias text.

Test objective:

Verify substituted alias text is re-tokenized and interpreted as fresh shell input.

What to test:

1. Basic alias replacement and execution
   - Define alias to command text and verify resulting command runs.

2. Token recognition restart at replacement start
   - Use alias value containing multiple tokens/operators and verify they are recognized normally as if typed directly.

3. Follow-on parsing continuity
   - Confirm tokens after the replaced TOKEN are still processed correctly in sequence.

text: If the value of the alias replacing the TOKEN ends in a <blank> that would be unquoted after substitution, and optionally if it ends in a <blank> that would be quoted after substitution, the shell shall check the next token in the input, if it is a TOKEN , for alias substitution; this process shall continue until a TOKEN is found that is not a valid alias or an alias value does not end in such a <blank>.

tests.test: alias trailing blank triggers expansion of next word
alias with trailing space chains to next word
alias trailing blank expands next word

testing_instructions:
If an alias replacement ends in an applicable <blank>, the shell continues checking the next TOKEN for alias substitution, repeating until conditions stop.

Test objective:

Verify trailing-blank chaining of alias substitutions.

What to test:

1. Single-step chain
   - Alias A ends with blank and next TOKEN B is alias; verify B is substituted.

2. Multi-step chain
   - Continue chaining through more than one alias and verify process stops at first non-alias or replacement without trailing blank.

3. Non-chain control
   - Confirm behavior when replacement does not end in qualifying blank does not force alias check of next token by this rule.

text: An implementation may defer the effect of a change to an alias but the change shall take effect no later than the completion of the currently executing complete_command (see 2.10 Shell Grammar ).

tests.test: alias trailing blank triggers expansion of next word

testing_instructions:
This clause allows deferred visibility of alias changes but requires they take effect no later than completion of the currently executing complete_command.

Test objective:

Verify alias updates become effective by the required boundary.

What to test:

1. Change within a complete_command
   - In contexts where timing matters, confirm effect may be deferred during current complete_command.

2. Post-boundary effect
   - Verify updated alias behavior is visible no later than next complete_command.

text: Changes to aliases shall not take effect out of order.
testing_instructions:
Alias changes shall not take effect out of order.

Test objective:

Verify multiple alias changes are observed in program order.

What to test:

1. Sequential alias redefinitions
   - Apply several alias changes and execute commands at checkpoints.

2. Order preservation
   - Confirm observed substitutions reflect the most recent in-order change, without reordering anomalies.

text: When used as specified by this volume of POSIX.1-2024, alias definitions shall not be inherited by separate invocations of the shell or by the utility execution environments invoked by the shell; see 2.13 Shell Execution Environment .

tests.test: alias in child shell does not leak to parent

testing_instructions: Verify alias definitions are not inherited by separate shell invocations by defining alias state in a child shell and confirming it is absent in the invoking shell environment.

text: The TOKEN is a valid alias name (see XBD 3.10 Alias Name).
untestable_reason: Requires dedicated alias-name validity matrix that is not yet modeled in current tests.
testing_instructions: Verify alias substitution eligibility requires TOKEN to be a syntactically valid alias name. Use valid and invalid alias-name-shaped tokens and confirm only valid alias-name tokens are considered for substitution.

text: An alias with that name is in effect.
testing_instructions: Verify alias substitution requires an alias to be currently in effect by defining an alias and confirming the token is replaced and executed.

text: The TOKEN did not either fully or, optionally, partially result from an alias substitution of the same alias name at any earlier recursion level.
untestable_reason: Portable recursion-level alias-substitution behavior is difficult to assert without shell-specific recursion controls.
testing_instructions: Verify the same alias name is not recursively re-applied at earlier recursion level in a way forbidden by this rule. Use self-referential/recursive alias setups and confirm behavior avoids prohibited re-substitution of same alias name.

text: Either the TOKEN is being considered for alias substitution because it follows an alias substitution whose replacement value ended with a <blank> (see below) or the TOKEN could be parsed as the command name word of a simple command (see 2.10 Shell Grammar), based on this TOKEN and the tokens (if any) that preceded it, but ignoring whether any subsequent characters would allow that.

tests.test: alias trailing blank triggers expansion of next word

testing_instructions: Verify tokens are considered for alias substitution either in command-name position or when enabled by trailing-blank chaining from a prior alias substitution.

text: Except that if the TOKEN meets the above conditions and would be recognized as a reserved word (see 2.4 Reserved Words) if it occurred in an appropriate place in the input, it is unspecified whether the TOKEN is subject to alias substitution.
untestable_reason: POSIX explicitly marks this case unspecified; no single portable expectation is valid.
testing_instructions: Treat this as unspecified behavior; do not enforce a single cross-shell conformance result.

text: If it does not add this <space>, it is unspecified whether the current token is delimited before token recognition is applied to the character (if any) that followed the TOKEN in the input.
untestable_reason: POSIX explicitly marks this no-added-space branch unspecified.
testing_instructions: Treat this as unspecified behavior; avoid strict pass/fail assertions for shells that choose not to add an extra space after alias replacement.

text: The following words shall be recognized as reserved words: ! { } case do done elif else esac fi for if in then until while.

tests.test: reserved words not recognized when quoted
reserved words work in correct positions

testing_instructions:
This requirement defines the core reserved-word set in 2.4. Test that these words are recognized as reserved words only in grammar-appropriate positions.

What to test:

1. Positive recognition for representative words
   - Verify words like if/then/fi and case/esac are recognized in syntactic positions where reserved words are valid.

2. Effect on parse structure
   - Confirm recognition changes parse behavior (for example building compound command syntax rather than invoking an external command with the same text).

3. Interaction with quoting
   - Pair with quoted forms to confirm reserved-word recognition depends on unquoted usage (full rule details in the companion recognition-context requirement).

Observable checks should focus on command behavior and parse outcomes (stdout/stderr/exit status).

text: This recognition shall only occur when none of the characters is quoted and when the word is used as: The first word of a command The first word following one of the reserved words other than case , for , or in The third word in a case command (only in is valid in this case) The third word in a for command (only in and do are valid in this case) See the grammar in 2.10 Shell Grammar .
testing_instructions:
This requirement constrains where reserved-word recognition occurs.

Test objective:

Verify reserved words are recognized only when unquoted and in the allowed grammar positions.

What to test:

1. Allowed positions
   - First word of a command.
   - First word following reserved words other than case/for/in.
   - Third word in case (only in valid there).
   - Third word in for (only in and do valid there).

2. Disallowed positions / quoted forms
   - Same lexemes when quoted or in other positions should not be treated as reserved words.

3. Grammar-consistent behavior
   - Confirm parse success/failure and resulting execution match the 2.10 grammar constraints.

text: When used in circumstances where reserved words are recognized (described above), the following words may be recognized as reserved words, in which case the results are unspecified except as described below for time : [[ ]] function namespace select time When the word time is recognized as a reserved word in circumstances where it would, if it were not a reserved word, be the command name (see 2.9.1.1 Order of Processing ) of a simple command that would execute the time utility in a manner other than one for which time states that the results are unspecified, the behavior shall be as specified for the time utility.

tests.test: quoted reserved word is not recognized
time reserved word measures and passes through output

testing_instructions:
This clause has two parts: (a) optional reserved-word recognition for [[, ]], function, namespace, select, and time with generally unspecified results, and (b) a defined rule for time when recognized as a reserved word in qualifying command-name circumstances.

Test objective:

Verify the defined time behavior while treating optional/unspecified keywords as non-portable.

What to test:

1. time reserved-word behavior (defined part)
   - In contexts where time is recognized as reserved word and would otherwise execute the time utility in a specified mode, verify behavior follows the time utility specification.

2. Optional keywords
   - For [[, ]], function, namespace, select (and time in optional-recognition sense), avoid strict conformance assertions across shells because recognition/results are unspecified.

3. Quoted vs unquoted context
   - Confirm quoted forms are not treated as reserved words by this clause.

text: When used in circumstances where reserved words are recognized (described above), all words whose final character is a <colon> (':') are reserved; their use in those circumstances produces unspecified results.
untestable_reason: POSIX explicitly states resulting behavior is unspecified.
testing_instructions: Treat this as unspecified behavior for conformance purposes; do not require a single portable result for colon-suffixed reserved words.

text: The shell shall process their values as characters only when performing operations that are described in this standard in terms of characters.

tests.test: ENV file processed for interactive shell

testing_instructions:
This requirement constrains interpretation mode for parameter values.

Test objective:

Verify parameter values are treated as character data only in operations specified in character terms, rather than being globally reinterpreted outside those operations.

What to test:

1. Character-oriented operations
   - Use operations that explicitly process characters and verify expected character-based behavior.

2. Non-character-oriented contexts
   - Confirm ordinary parameter storage/transport does not impose extra character reinterpretation semantics beyond what the invoked operation defines.

3. Observable outcomes
   - Assert via stdout/stderr/exit behavior of commands using parameter values.

text: A parameter can be denoted by a name, a number, or one of the special characters listed in 2.5.2 Special Parameters.

tests.test: parameters can be referenced by name number and special character

testing_instructions: Verify parameter notation forms by exercising named parameters, positional numeric parameters, and special parameters in one script.

text: A variable is a parameter denoted by a name.

tests.test: variable is parameter referenced by name

testing_instructions: Verify that assigning and expanding a name-denoted parameter behaves as variable access.

text: A parameter is set if it has an assigned value (null is a valid value).

tests.test: null assignment still means parameter is set

testing_instructions: Verify assigned null values still count as set parameters, while unassigned parameters remain unset.

text: Once a variable is set, it can only be unset by using the unset special built-in command.

tests.test: assigned variable remains set until unset is used

testing_instructions: Verify a variable remains set after reassignment (including null assignment) and transitions to unset state when unset is executed.

text: Parameters can contain arbitrary byte sequences, except for the null byte.
testing_instructions: Verify parameters preserve representative non-null bytes (for example tab and newline) through assignment and expansion.

text: The digits denoting the positional parameters shall always be interpreted as a decimal value, even if there is a leading zero.

tests.test: $@ with no positional params generates zero fields

testing_instructions:
This requirement in 2.5.1 states positional-parameter digits are interpreted as decimal, including with leading zero.

Test objective:

Verify numeric interpretation of positional parameter references is decimal, not octal, when digits include leading zero.

What to test:

1. Decimal interpretation with leading zero
   - Use positional expansions that include leading zeros (for example ${08} where valid context applies) and verify they target decimal parameter index.

2. Distinguish from special parameter 0 behavior
   - Confirm cases like $01 behave as special-parameter 0 followed by literal digit where brace syntax is not used, consistent with POSIX examples.

3. Observable checks
   - Assert by comparing expanded values against known argument lists.

text: When a positional parameter with more than one digit is specified, the application shall enclose the digits in braces (see 2.6.2 Parameter Expansion ).
testing_instructions:
This requirement mandates braces when referencing positional parameters with more than one digit.

Test objective:

Verify portable usage requires brace form for multi-digit positional parameters.

What to test:

1. Braced multi-digit expansion
   - Use ${10} (and similar) and verify it expands to the intended positional parameter.

2. Non-braced contrast
   - Compare with $10 and verify it is interpreted as $1 followed by literal 0, matching POSIX examples.

3. Observable checks
   - Provide argument vectors where the two forms produce distinct outputs to confirm parsing difference.

text: A positional parameter is a parameter denoted by a decimal representation of a positive integer.

tests.test: positional parameters use positive integer indices

testing_instructions: Verify positional parameters are denoted by positive decimal integers by setting arguments and expanding $1, $2, and similar forms.

text: Positional parameters are initially assigned when the shell is invoked (see sh), temporarily replaced when a shell function is invoked (see 2.9.5 Function Definition Command), and can be reassigned with the set special built-in command.

tests.test: positional parameters follow invocation function and set lifecycle

testing_instructions: Verify positional-parameter lifecycle across shell invocation, set-based reassignment, and temporary function-call replacement/restoration.

text: Listed below are the special parameters and the values to which they shall expand.
testing_instructions: Umbrella clause for special parameters in 2.5.2. Verify that each listed special parameter expands according to its dedicated rule; this entry is validated through coverage of the per-parameter requirements below.

text: When the expansion occurs in a context where field splitting will be performed, any empty fields may be discarded and each of the non-empty fields shall be further split as described in 2.6.5 Field Splitting .

tests.test: ENV set to valid file does not crash non-interactive shell

testing_instructions: For special parameters that initially produce fields from positional parameters in field-splitting contexts, verify empty fields may be discarded and non-empty fields are split per 2.6.5. Use crafted positional values containing IFS separators and confirm resulting field set.

text: If one of these conditions is true, the initial fields shall be retained as separate fields, except that if the parameter being expanded was embedded within a word, the first field shall be joined with the beginning part of the original word and the last field shall be joined with the end part of the original word.

tests.suite: Maybe-Builtin Utilities (true, false, test, pwd, printf)

tests.test: $@ with no positional params generates zero fields
# in middle of word is not a comment

testing_instructions: When the specified double-quote conditions for '@' hold, verify initial fields remain separate, with first/last field joining behavior when embedded within surrounding word text. Assert resulting argument boundaries precisely.

text: If there are no positional parameters, the expansion of '@' shall generate zero fields, even when '@' is within double-quotes; however, if the expansion is embedded within a word which contains one or more other parts that expand to a quoted null string, these null string(s) shall still produce an empty field, except that if the other parts are all within the same double-quotes as the '@' , it is unspecified whether the result is zero fields or one empty field.

tests.suite: Maybe-Builtin Utilities (true, false, test, pwd, printf)

tests.test: $@ with no positional params generates zero fields

testing_instructions: Verify '@' with no positional parameters yields zero fields, including double-quoted contexts, and cover the quoted-null-string embedding exception/unspecified branch without enforcing unspecified outcomes.

text: When the expansion occurs in a context where field splitting will not be performed, the initial fields shall be joined to form a single field with the value of each parameter separated by the first character of the IFS variable if IFS contains at least one character, or separated by a <space> if IFS is unset, or with no separation if IFS is set to a null string.

tests.test: quoted $* is single string, quoted $@ is distinct args
set -u fails on length of unset variable

testing_instructions: In contexts without field splitting, verify '*' joins positional parameters into one field using IFS first char, or space when IFS unset, or no separator when IFS is null. Assert exact joined output.

text: The command name (parameter 0) shall not be counted in the number given by '#' because it is a special parameter, not a positional parameter.
testing_instructions: Verify '#' reports the decimal count of positional parameters and excludes parameter 0. Use controlled argument vectors and assert exact numeric stdout.

text: If this pipeline terminated, the status value shall be its exit status; otherwise, the status value shall be the same as the exit status that would have resulted if the pipeline had been terminated by a signal with the same number as the signal that stopped it.

tests.test: environment variable is visible via env

testing_instructions: For '?' status semantics, verify terminated pipeline cases map to the pipeline exit status. Where signal-stopped pipeline behavior is optional/interactive-specific, avoid strict portable assertions unless environment guarantees it.

text: The value of the special parameter '?' shall be set to 0 during initialization of the shell.

tests.test: ENV set to valid file does not crash non-interactive shell

testing_instructions: Verify special parameter '?' is initialized to 0 at shell startup before commands run. Use minimal shell invocations and assert initial value.

text: When a subshell environment is created, the value of the special parameter '?' from the invoking shell environment shall be preserved in the subshell.

text: In a subshell (see 2.13 Shell Execution Environment ), '$' shall expand to the same value as that of the current shell.

tests.test: ENV file processed for interactive shell

testing_instructions: Verify '$' expands to same value in subshell as in current shell, per 2.13 subshell semantics. Compare parent and subshell expansions directly.

text: '@' expands to the positional parameters, starting from one, initially producing one field for each positional parameter that is set.
testing_instructions: Core '@' baseline: positional parameters map to fields in order from 1. Validate indirectly via detailed @ expansion rules and field-shape tests.

text: '*' expands to the positional parameters, starting from one, initially producing one field for each positional parameter that is set.
testing_instructions: Core '*' baseline: positional parameters are source fields in order from 1, with final behavior determined by splitting context rules.

text: '#' expands to the shortest representation of the decimal number of positional parameters.
testing_instructions: Verify '#' yields decimal positional-parameter count string; treated as baseline companion to the explicit 'exclude parameter 0' clause.

text: '?' expands to the shortest representation of the decimal exit status of the pipeline executed from the current shell execution environment that most recently either terminated or, optionally but only if the shell is interactive and job control is enabled, was stopped by a signal.
testing_instructions: Composite '?' baseline clause; validate through narrower requirements for initialization, pipeline termination mapping, and subshell preservation.

text: '-' (hyphen) expands to the current option flags as single-letter option names concatenated into a string as specified on invocation, by set, or implicitly by the shell; it is unspecified whether -c and -s are included in '$-'.
testing_instructions: Validate '$-' option-flag expansion cautiously; do not assert -c/-s inclusion because POSIX marks that portion unspecified.

text: '$' expands to the shortest representation of the decimal process ID of the invoked shell.
testing_instructions: Verify '$' yields current shell PID string in decimal form; treat as baseline companion to subshell-preservation rule.

text: '!' expands to the shortest representation of the decimal process ID associated with the most recent asynchronous AND-OR list executed from the current shell execution environment, or the last command in the most recently resumed job-control background pipeline via bg, whichever is most recent.

testing_instructions: Async/job-control dependent '!'-PID behavior; defer strict conformance assertion unless harness provides deterministic background-job control timing.

text: If an AND-OR list is terminated by the control operator <ampersand> ( '&' ), the shell shall execute the AND-OR list asynchronously in a subshell environment.

This subshell shall execute in the background; that is, the shell shall not wait for the subshell to terminate before executing the next command (if any); if there are no further commands to execute, the shell shall not wait for the subshell to terminate before exiting.
If job control is enabled (see set , -m ), the AND-OR list shall become a job-control background job and a job number shall be assigned to it.

If job control is disabled, the AND-OR list may become a non-job-control background job, in which case a job number shall be assigned to it; if no job number is assigned it shall become a background command but not a background job.
The process ID associated with the asynchronous AND-OR list shall become known in the current shell execution environment; see 2.13 Shell Execution Environment .
This process ID shall remain known until any one of the following occurs (and, unless otherwise specified, may continue to remain known after it occurs).

If, and only if, job control is disabled, the standard input for the subshell in which an asynchronous AND-OR list is executed shall initially be assigned to an open file description that behaves as if /dev/null had been opened for reading only.
This initial assignment shall be overridden by any explicit redirection of standard input within the AND-OR list.

If the shell is interactive and the asynchronous AND-OR list became a background job, the job number and the process ID associated with the job shall be written to standard error using the format: "[%d] %d\n", < job-number >, < process-id > If the shell is interactive and the asynchronous AND-OR list did not become a background job, the process ID associated with the asynchronous AND-OR list shall be written to standard error in an unspecified format.

text: Subsequently, the shell shall change the foreground process group associated with its controlling terminal when a foreground job is running as noted in the description below.

When job control is enabled, the shell shall create one or more jobs when it executes a list (see 2.9.3 Lists ) that has one of the following forms: A single asynchronous AND-OR list One or more sequentially executed AND-OR lists followed by at most one asynchronous AND-OR list For the purposes of job control, a list that includes more than one asynchronous AND-OR list shall be treated as if it were split into multiple separate lists, each ending with an asynchronous AND-OR list.
